description: Food can satisfy hunger, and an account can satisfy a stated criterion. 'heno' says that something meets a need, desire, condition, or test to the relevant degree. 'henoi' (ENOUGH) judges an amount against a requirement, while 'korei' (fulfill) carries an undertaking or purpose into actuality.
articulatory_notes: Let unvoiced air pass through the open glottis for 'h' and move directly into stressed middle-height front 'e'. Begin 'no' at a voiced dental contact, carry its resonance through the nose, and round the lips into middle-height back 'o'.
sound_symbolism: Open breath narrows into nasal contact and settles on rounded 'o'. The movement has the bodily ease of pressure released when enough arrives.
usage_notes: The subject supplies whatever satisfies; the need, criterion, or person affected is the object. When that object is a person, context tells whether a need or desire has been met. Noun 'heno' is satisfaction.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Satisfaction can mark the point at which a need has enough without claiming that desire has ended forever. 'heno' helps a speaker notice sufficiency as an actual relation.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A request or agreement can be checked against the need it was meant to answer. 'heno' reports that fit without deciding whether every participant has the same need.

semantic_domains.activity: a condition or criterion met by an action or result
semantic_domains.cognition: judgement that stated requirements have been met
semantic_domains.emotion: a desire or felt need brought to satisfaction
semantic_domains.physical: bodily need met by food, water, rest, or another provision
